one_line_takeaway: AdapTools shows that closing the loop between an attacker LLM, a chain-of-thought failure analyzer, and a Markov-chain-guided task-relevant tool selector roughly doubles indirect-prompt-injection success rates against both commercial and open-source tool-using LLM agents (2.13x on commercial models, up to 44.5-49.0% ASR on open-source models) and cuts SOTA defense effectiveness in half, using a newly released 3,691-trajectory / 277-attack-tool benchmark (IPI-3K).
key_finding: From Table 3 (No Defense rows, per-model averages across the four defense conditions incl. no-defense): on GPT-4.1, AdapTools reaches 18.5% average ASR vs. best/average baseline under 8% (best single baseline configuration reported as 14.5% in the abstract); on Gemini-2.5-Flash, 25.9% vs. 9.2% baseline average; on DeepSeek-R1, 13.5% vs. 6.7% best baseline. Averaged across the three commercial LLMs the paper reports a 2.13x ASR improvement over the best baseline. On open-source models, average ASR is higher: 44.5% on Qwen3-8B (up from 52.7%->60.6% ASR in the ablation's w/ selection row, and utility-preserving relative to baselines), 49.0% on Mistral-8B (attributed to weaker grounding, only 36.0% Benign Utility), and 31.4% on LLaMA-3.1-8B (which shows severe utility degradation under attack despite lower ASR); the paper states open-source models average 58.1% ASR vs. baseline's 38.2% (per the abstract). Against SOTA defenses, AdapTools ASR only drops by roughly 2x (e.g., GPT-4.1: 26.1% no-defense -> 13.3% MELON -> 16.1% Pi-Detector) whereas baseline attacks drop by at least 3x. Ablation (Table 4): the tool-selection (Attack Enhancement) module alone improves ASR by +4.7 points on GPT-4.1 (21.4%->26.1%) and +7.9 points on Qwen3-8B (52.7%->60.6%), with UA changes of +1.0 and -3.8 respectively. Transferability (Fig. 7, InjectAgent dataset): AdapTools outperforms AutoHijacker by +3.6% ASR on GPT-4.1 and +6.5% on Qwen3-8B, though absolute ASR on InjectAgent is lower than on the authors' own IPI-3K, which the paper attributes to LLM vendors patching previously known/public attack cases. A motivational finding (RQ1, pre-method): reasoning-enabled Qwen3-8B-thinking shows lower ASR under Combined Attack (19.4%) than non-thinking Qwen3-8B (26.1%) and non-thinking LLaMA-3.1-8B (32.8%), attributed to explicit CoT cross-checking of tool calls against user intent.
core_contribution: The paper introduces AdapTools, an adaptive indirect prompt injection (IPI) attack framework for tool-augmented (MCP-integrated) LLM agents, with two components: (1) an Adaptive Attack Module that iteratively generates, tests, and refines attack prompts against a live victim agent using an attacker LLM (e.g., GPT-5) plus a Failure Analyzer that reads the victim's chain-of-thought to diagnose why an attack was rejected (categorized as Red Herring, Security Risk, or Unrelated Information), archiving successful strategies into a Strategy Library and periodically distilling/compressing that library via text embeddings + K-means clustering and an ASR-based pruning threshold; and (2) an Attack Enhancement module that selects a task-relevant malicious tool via a first-order Markov transition matrix over benign tool-use trajectories combined with cosine similarity between candidate attack tools and the predicted next benign tool, so the injected tool call is both temporally plausible and semantically aligned with the user's task (a grey-box setting where the adversary can observe the most recently invoked tool). The authors also construct and release a new benchmark, IPI-3K, consisting of 3,691 benign agent trajectories and 277 high-authority 'attack tools' (categorized into data leakage, financial loss, and system harm, risk-scored 0-10 by commercial LLMs), built by consolidating/reorganizing an existing benchmark (AgentAlign, Zhang et al. 2025a).
threat_model: The agent queries external MCP/API servers to retrieve public content; the returned observation may contain adversary-crafted malicious instructions concatenated into otherwise-benign content. Two attacker types are considered: (i) grey-box 'MCP server controllers' who can observe partial trajectory information, specifically the most recently invoked tool, and (ii) black-box 'third-party adversaries' who only broadcast malicious instructions publicly with no access to the agent's internal state or task. The adversary's objective is formalized as maximizing the probability the agent's next action is a target malicious tool f_a, by optimizing an injected prompt p_a appended to the tool observation (o~_t = o_t ⊕ p_a).
limitations: The paper does not state explicit limitations in a dedicated section (none present in the paper beyond the Impact Statement). Notable gaps observable from the text itself: (1) AutoHijacker is a self-replication ('* our own replication of the work as the code is not open source'), so its baseline numbers are not independently verifiable against the original authors' reported results. (2) The attack-enhancement/grey-box tool-selection ablation (Table 4) is run on only two models (one commercial, one open-source) 'due to the high costs of API calling,' limiting generality of that specific result. (3) IPI-3K's benign trajectories are derived by 'consolidation and reorganization' of an existing benchmark (AgentAlign) rather than being independently sourced, so its novelty is primarily in the attack-tool pool (277 tools) and scale rather than fully original task data. (4) ASR on the more standard InjectAgent benchmark is markedly lower than on the authors' own IPI-3K benchmark, which the paper attributes to defenses being patched against known/public attacks -- this is a plausible but unverified post-hoc explanation and raises the question of how much of AdapTools' advantage on IPI-3K reflects the benchmark's own composition versus general attack potency. (5) Even the best defense (MELON/Pi-Detector) only reduces AdapTools' ASR by roughly half, meaning residual non-trivial attack success remains against SOTA defenses across all six models tested.

datasets_used: IPI-3K (introduced in this paper: 3,691 benign agent trajectories consolidated/reorganized from AgentAlign (Zhang et al., 2025a); 277 high-authority attack tools spanning data leakage, financial loss, and system harm, risk-scored 0-10 by GPT-4o/DeepSeek-R1)
InjectAgent (Zhan et al., 2024) -- 510 test cases, 27 attack tools (Table 2)
AgentDojo (Debenedetti et al., 2024) -- 629 test cases, 30 attack tools (Table 2)
quantitative_results: Abstract/overall: AdapTools achieves 2.13x ASR improvement over best baseline on commercial LLMs while degrading utility by 1.78x factor
Commercial LLMs average ASR: AdapTools 18.5% (GPT-4.1), 25.9% (Gemini-2.5-Flash), 13.5% (DeepSeek-R1) vs. baseline averages 5.0-11.1% and best single baseline 14.5% (abstract)
Open-source LLMs average ASR: 44.5% (Qwen3-8B), 49.0% (Mistral-8B), 31.4% (LLaMA-3.1-8B); paper-level summary: 58.1% avg ASR (AdapTools) vs 38.2% avg ASR (baseline) on locally deployed LLMs
Defense robustness (GPT-4.1, no-defense -> MELON -> Pi-Detector): 26.1% -> 13.3% -> 16.1% ASR for AdapTools vs. baseline attacks dropping >=3x
Iteration sweep (Fig. 8): ASR ~35% at 1 iteration, marked key point (5 iterations, 0.81 ASR), diminishing returns at 6-7 iterations; K=5 chosen as default
Transferability on InjectAgent (Fig. 7): AdapTools beats AutoHijacker by +3.6% ASR on GPT-4.1 and +6.5% ASR on Qwen3-8B
RQ1 motivational result: Combined Attack ASR -- Qwen3-8B-thinking 19.4% vs. Qwen3-8B non-thinking 26.1% vs. LLaMA-3.1-8B non-thinking 32.8%
IPI-3K benchmark composition (Table 2): 3,691 test cases / 277 attack tools vs. AgentDojo's 629/30 and InjectAgent's 510/27
Failure-mode breakdown on Qwen3-8B under IPI-3K IPI attacks (Fig. 2): Security Risk 29.3%, Red Herring 24.3%, No Functions 20.2%, Unrelated 8.7%, Arguments Error 9.9%, Others 7.6%
baselines_compared: Ignore Instruction (Schulhoff et al., 2023) -- prefix-based prompt attack
InjectAgent (Zhan et al., 2024) -- used both as an attack method baseline and, separately, as a dataset name
AutoHijacker (Liu et al., 2025) -- authors' own replication, code not open source
Defenses compared against: MELON (Zhu et al., 2025) and Pi-Detector (Debenedetti et al., 2024)
